meta.title: Use advanced programming techniques to develop a computer program
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves using advanced programming techniques to develop a computer program.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

omis.description: Program demonstrably performs the specified task from the assessment brief.

positive_signals: Run logs showing successful execution of the specified task
Screenshots demonstrating program output matching requirements
Screencast evidencing required task behavior
negative_signals: Code that fails to run or execute the task
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Demonstration must be direct and observable; verbal claims or intent statements are insufficient.

success_examples: code was able to be run
screencasts confirmed function of the program

omis.description: Implements at least two advanced programming techniques during development.

positive_signals: Code snippets implementing object-oriented classes
Code demonstrating recursion or complex algorithms
Use of advanced libraries or frameworks in code
negative_signals: Mere verbal claims of using techniques
Plans or descriptions without actual code implementation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Techniques must be evidenced in the code; claims or plans alone do not count.

omis.description: Code is clearly laid out (consistent indentation, logical sections, whitespace for readability).

positive_signals: Consistent indentation across all code files
Logical grouping of code into sections
Appropriate use of whitespace for readability
negative_signals: Single header comment without overall structure
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Layout must be consistent and structured; a single comment cannot compensate for poor formatting.

omis.description: Code includes comments that document purpose and behaviour of sections.

positive_signals: Comments explaining the purpose of functions
Comments describing the behavior of code sections
Multi-line comments for complex logic
negative_signals: Absence of comments in key sections
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Comments must document purpose and behavior, not just repeat the code.

omis.description: Testing evidence is organised to show inputs used and whether expected outputs occurred.

positive_signals: Table mapping inputs to expected and actual outputs
Structured test logs with input-output pairs
Organized evidence showing test results
negative_signals: No mapping between inputs and outputs
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Evidence must be organized and structured; informal claims are insufficient.

success_examples: shows each input of the program being tested
whether expected outputs were generated or not

omis.description: Testing demonstrates correct behaviour on typical/expected inputs; includes at least one debugging instance.

positive_signals: Test cases showing expected and actual outputs for typical inputs
Before-and-after debugging example
Demonstration of correct behavior on expected cases
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must include at least one debugging instance; typical tests alone are insufficient.

omis.description: Uses appropriate names and comments that describe code function and behaviour.

positive_signals: Variable names reflecting their role (e.g., 'score' instead of 'x')
Comments describing code function and behavior
Descriptive identifiers throughout the code
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Names and comments must be descriptive and appropriate; generic ones fail the criteria.

omis.description: Follows common conventions for the chosen programming language throughout.

positive_signals: Consistent naming conventions (e.g., camelCase in JavaScript)
Use of language-specific structures and idioms
Uniform style throughout the codebase
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Conventions must be followed consistently; mixed styles are insufficient.

success_examples: common conventions for the chosen programming language

omis.description: Testing includes relevant boundary cases demonstrating correct behaviour on and around boundaries.

positive_signals: Test cases at exact boundary values
Tests below and above boundaries
Expected versus actual results for boundary tests
negative_signals: Lack of test evidence for boundaries
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Tests must be concrete and include results; mere mentions are insufficient.

success_examples: lack of boundary testing for Merit
omis.source_ref: Moderator commentary – Practices that need strengthening

omis.description: Program constitutes a well-structured, logical response to the specified task.

positive_signals: Modular functions with clear responsibilities
Logical organization of code aligned to task
Coherent structure without tangles
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Program must be well-structured and logical; mere functionality is insufficient.

omis.description: Comprehensive testing includes invalid and unexpected inputs, evidencing robustness.

positive_signals: Test cases for invalid inputs with handling
Tests for unexpected inputs demonstrating robustness
Organized evidence of input handling
negative_signals: Lack of invalid or unexpected input tests
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must include invalid and unexpected inputs; typical-only testing is insufficient for Excellence.

success_examples: Comprehensive testing is required for Excellence.
include details of invalid and unexpected inputs
omis.source_ref: Moderator commentary – Practices that need strengthening

omis.description: Testing evidence supports that the program is robust under adverse or unexpected conditions.

positive_signals: Tests showing graceful failure under adverse conditions
Evidence of recovery from unexpected inputs
Explicit links from test results to robustness claims
negative_signals: No demonstration of adverse condition handling
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must explicitly link test results to robustness; unsupported claims are insufficient.

omis.source_ref: Moderator commentary – Practices that need strengthening
